label: Vocal Command
definition: The ability to project voice with authority, clarity, and controlled volume so that the speaker fills the space and commands listener attention without electronic amplification.
observable_markers: Voice carries to the back of a large room without shouting
Volume is modulated — quiet when intimate, loud when rousing
Tone does not waver under pressure
Voice does not go thin or reedy under stress

examples.description: Sustained vocal power across 17 minutes in outdoor heat, with dynamic range from whisper to full-throated call

examples.speech_title: We Shall Fight on the Beaches
examples.description: Radio voice that projected gravitas and resolve through medium, not volume

label: Strategic Pause
definition: Deliberate use of silence as a rhetorical tool — to create anticipation, let a key idea land, signal gravity, or reset audience attention.
observable_markers: Speaker pauses before the most important word or phrase
Silence is held for 2–4 seconds without visible discomfort
Pause follows a major claim, not a minor transition
Audience leans in or goes quiet during the pause

examples.description: Repeated use of pause before 'Yes. We. Can.' to create rhythm and emotional space
Extended pause before revealing 'an iPod, a phone, and an internet communicator — are you getting it?'

label: Cadence Control
definition: The ability to vary speaking pace — slowing for gravity, accelerating for momentum, using rhythm to guide emotion and comprehension.
observable_markers: Speaker deliberately slows on key words
Sentences vary in speed — not monotone
Fast passages create urgency; slow passages create weight
Natural-feeling rhythm, not mechanical

examples.description: Cadence built like music — each repetition of 'I have a dream' slower and heavier than the last
Poetic pacing that mirrored the weight and lightness of each line

label: Rhythmic Repetition
definition: Deliberate reuse of words, phrases, or sentence structures to create emotional force, memorability, and a cumulative building effect.
observable_markers: Repeated anchor phrase builds in intensity across uses
Structure is consistent — same syntax each repetition
Audience begins to anticipate and internally complete the phrase
Phrase becomes the takeaway or slogan of the speech

examples.description: 'We shall fight on the beaches, we shall fight on the landing grounds...' — six-part anaphora
'I have a dream' and 'Let freedom ring' — two parallel anaphoric sequences

label: Metaphor and Imagery
definition: Using concrete images, comparisons, and figurative language to make abstract ideas visible, memorable, and emotionally resonant.
observable_markers: Abstractions are consistently illustrated with physical images
Metaphors are fresh, not clichéd
Imagery appeals to multiple senses
Central metaphor is consistent across the speech

examples.description: Sustained use of financial metaphor ('bad check', 'promissory note') to make injustice concrete
The 'Iron Curtain' itself — one of the most impactful metaphors in political history

label: Story Arc Control
definition: Ability to structure a speech with a recognizable narrative arc — problem, tension, turning point, resolution — that holds attention and drives toward a conclusion.
observable_markers: Speech has a clear beginning, middle, and end
A central conflict or problem is established early
Tension builds across the speech
Resolution or call-to-action emerges naturally from the story

examples.description: Opened in grief, moved through meaning-making, closed with aspiration — complete emotional arc in 4 minutes
Personal story as the spine: researcher who studied vulnerability becomes the subject

label: Audience Connection
definition: Ability to make individual listeners feel seen, spoken to directly, and emotionally included in the speech — regardless of audience size.
observable_markers: Speaker uses 'you' and 'we' language inclusively
Eye contact feels direct, not scanning
References to shared experience resonate with room
Audience visibly responds — heads nod, posture opens

examples.description: Famous for making individuals in large crowds feel personally addressed
Systematically included every demographic in the room through direct address

label: Intellectual Clarity
definition: Ability to explain complex ideas with precision, structure, and simplicity — making the difficult accessible without losing accuracy.
observable_markers: Concepts are defined before being used
Arguments follow a logical sequence
Complex ideas are illustrated with examples
No jargon without explanation

examples.description: Explained quantum electrodynamics to a general audience through cumulative analogies
Translated cosmic scale into human emotional terms with precise language

label: Crisis Reassurance
definition: Ability to project calm authority in moments of national or collective crisis — reducing panic while acknowledging gravity.
observable_markers: Voice is steady even when delivering terrible news
Speaker acknowledges the fear without amplifying it
Language of 'we' and shared fate predominates
Provides a frame for action or hope alongside acknowledgment of loss

examples.description: Explained bank closures to a frightened public so simply that the panic stopped
Named the cost without hiding it, then channeled fear into determination

label: Humor and Timing
definition: Strategic use of wit, irony, self-deprecation, or absurdity to build rapport, lower resistance, disarm critics, and create memorable moments.
observable_markers: Punchline lands after setup — not simultaneously
Speaker does not telegraph or laugh at own joke
Humor is appropriate to context and audience
Jokes create connection, not discomfort

examples.description: Mastered self-deprecating political humor with perfect timing and deadpan delivery
'There you go again' and the 'youth and inexperience' line defused Mondale attack with a smile

label: Plain Language
definition: Commitment to using everyday vocabulary and short sentences so that any audience can follow — without simplifying the idea itself.
observable_markers: Sentences average under 20 words
Words are commonly known (Flesch reading ease above 60)
Technical terms replaced with everyday equivalents
Idea is not dumbed down — only the vocabulary is simplified

examples.description: 267 words, almost all monosyllabic Anglo-Saxon — one of the densest ideas ever encoded in the simplest language
Complex constitutional argument made comprehensible to all through plain declarative language

label: Vision Framing
definition: Ability to articulate a compelling image of a desired future in terms concrete enough to inspire action but open enough to invite individual identification.
observable_markers: Future is described in sensory, not bureaucratic terms
Vision is specific enough to be recognizable
Listener can see themselves in the future described
Vision implies a path — not just a destination

examples.description: Each 'dream' sub-vision was specific, geographic, and rooted in the American founding
'We choose to go to the moon in this decade' — specific, bounded, testable vision

label: Authority Projection
definition: The non-verbal and verbal signals that cause audiences to grant the speaker expert or moral authority — posture, confidence, specific claims, and source citation.
observable_markers: Speaker stands or sits with physical stillness and uprightness
Does not hedge unnecessarily — states claims directly
Cites sources or expertise when appropriate
Does not seek approval from audience

examples.description: Achieved authority through factual precision and refusal to perform emotion
'The lady's not for turning' — direct, unhedged, no seeking of approval

label: Emotional Resonance
definition: The ability to move an audience emotionally — creating genuine feelings of hope, grief, pride, or urgency through language, story, and delivery — not performance.
observable_markers: Audience members visibly moved (tears, visible tension)
Emotion in speaker is real, not performed
Emotional moments are earned — not rushed
Emotion serves the argument — does not replace it

examples.description: 27 years of consequence accepted with complete calm — the restraint made the emotion devastating
Personal survival story made universal without self-pity

label: Evidence-Based Persuasion
definition: Building arguments on verifiable facts, data, and cited expertise — making emotional and moral claims credible through factual grounding.
observable_markers: Claims are followed by evidence within seconds
Data is made concrete and relatable (not just cited)
Sources are named — not vague ('experts say')
Counterarguments are acknowledged and addressed

examples.description: Every claim about climate grounded in specific science and date-anchored data

examples.speech_title: The Best Stats You've Ever Seen (TED)
examples.description: Made statistics into narrative by animating them through time and human story

label: Body Language Control
definition: Deliberate use of gesture, posture, movement, and stillness to reinforce verbal message and project confidence.
observable_markers: Gestures are purposeful — not nervous fidgeting
Posture is open and grounded
Movement is deliberate — no pacing
Physical stillness matches moments of maximum importance

examples.description: Used a single pointing gesture deliberately; hands were still when they needed to be
Minimal gesture, deliberate movement across stage — stillness amplified his words

label: Debate Sharpness
definition: Ability to listen precisely to an opponent's argument, identify its weakest point, and rebut it with speed, logic, and composure.
observable_markers: Rebuttal begins from the opponent's actual words
Attack is logical, not personal
Response is concise — no rambling
Maintains composure under attack

examples.description: Listened completely, then attacked the precise pivot point — not a caricature
Mastered the pre-emptive rebuttal — addressing objections before they were raised

label: Direct Address
definition: Speaking to a specific person, institution, or group in the audience — with the implied presence of that target — to create urgency and intimacy in a large setting.
observable_markers: Speaker uses 'you' to address a named person or group
Target of address feels confronted, not just mentioned
Specific behavioral expectation is named
Other listeners feel the intensity of the addressed moment

examples.description: 'Mr. Gorbachev, tear down this wall!' — direct address to a named world leader
Addressed Taliban directly ('you cannot silence us') in a United Nations setting

label: Memorability
definition: Ability to craft lines, images, or moments so distinctive that they survive across time — quoted decades or centuries later.
observable_markers: One phrase per speech can stand alone and mean something
Key phrase is short enough to repeat from memory
Image or formulation is original — not borrowed
Phrase works as a headline without its context

examples.description: 'Ask not what your country can do for you — ask what you can do for your country'
'An iron curtain has descended across the continent' — three words became geopolitical doctrine

label: Call to Action
definition: Closing with a specific, urgent, achievable demand for audience behavior — converting emotional engagement into commitment and movement.
observable_markers: Speech ends with an explicit ask, not just inspiration
Action named is specific and doable
Urgency is felt — 'now' is implied
Speaker names who should do what

examples.description: Ended with implied legislative demand — not inspiration but indictment with implied action
Always closed with 'fight' — specific verb, specific stake, specific ask

label: Teaching Simplicity
definition: Ability to build understanding in a listener from nothing — using sequence, analogy, and repetition to produce genuine comprehension.
observable_markers: New concept builds on the one before it — never assumes
Analogies are drawn from familiar experience
Key point is repeated in different words across the speech
Listener leaves knowing something they did not before

examples.description: Used rubber band, ice water, and jiggling atoms to explain quantum mechanics on national television
Built a scaffold of analogy that let viewers ascend from their kitchen to deep time

label: Prophetic Tone
definition: Speaking as a moral witness to the present — naming injustice, corruption, or danger with authority that transcends personal interest, as if summoned to speak the truth others cannot.
observable_markers: Speaker appears to be speaking to history, not the room
Language has gravity beyond argument — it has verdict
No hedging or softening of the central moral claim
Tone is sorrowful, not angry — or righteous, not bitter

examples.speech_title: What to the Slave is the Fourth of July?
examples.description: Spoke from outside the celebration with the authority of lived suffering — the tone made argument unnecessary
Spoke to white moderate clergy as a disappointed friend — sorrow amplifying moral force

label: Cross-Cultural Bridge
definition: Ability to speak to audiences of different cultural backgrounds, languages, and reference frames — finding universal human frames that transcend group identity.
observable_markers: Speaker draws on multiple cultural traditions without appropriating
Examples and metaphors work across cultural contexts
Language acknowledges diversity without performing it
Speech is translatable without losing meaning

examples.speech_title: Long Walk to Freedom press conference
examples.description: Spoke to white South Africans, Black South Africans, and the world simultaneously — different messages in one register
Maintained cultural neutrality across the most diverse audiences on earth

label: Ethical Restraint
definition: Choosing not to use available rhetorical power for manipulation, fear, or scapegoating — deploying influence with responsibility and proportionality.
observable_markers: Does not exploit emotional vulnerability of audience
Names adversaries accurately — not with dehumanizing language
Makes claims it can back — does not overclaim for effect
Does not exploit grief, fear, or group identity to bypass reason

examples.description: 'With malice toward none, with charity for all' — maximum moral authority through maximum restraint
Governed every word to ensure South Africa's white minority heard inclusion, not retribution

label: Moral Clarity
definition: Ability to name right and wrong directly, without equivocation — using moral language that is precise and backed by principle rather than sentiment.
observable_markers: Uses words like 'wrong', 'unjust', 'unacceptable' with specificity
Names the moral principle being violated
Does not moralize — names and moves
Moral claim is backed by evidence or principle, not just feeling

examples.description: 'It is not power that corrupts but fear' — moral diagnosis in one sentence

examples.speech_title: What to the Slave is the Fourth of July?
examples.description: Named slavery as 'barbarous' and 'savage' with calm precision — not rhetorical performance
